props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Research Checkpoint Immunotherapy Response Rates: Why Do Hot Tumors Respond and Cold Tumors Not?

beats.narration_text: Humanitarians AI. Hot vs cold tumors — a 15-fold gap.

props.command: Research Checkpoint Immunotherapy Response Rates: Why Do Hot Tumors Respond and Cold Tumors Not?
props.segment: Research Checkpoint Immunotherapy Response Rates: Why Do Hot Tumors Respond and Cold Tumors Not?
output: Hot vs cold tumors — a 15-fold gap.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Research Checkpoint Immunotherapy Response Rates: Why Do Hot Tumors Respond and Cold Tumors Not? belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.command: Research Checkpoint Immunotherapy Response Rates: Why Do Hot Tumors Respond and Cold Tumors Not?
props.segment: Research Checkpoint Immunotherapy Response Rates: Why Do Hot Tumors Respond and Cold Tumors Not?
output: Hot vs cold tumors — a 15-fold gap.

beats.narration_text: Anti-PD-1 works in sixty-five to seventy-five percent of Hodgkin lymphoma and under five percent of pancreatic cancer. Both are cancer. The fifteen-fold difference in response rate reflects a biological difference — and the question is whether it is addressable.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- problem card: 15-fold gap in response rates; hot vs cold tumor biology

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to compile anti-PD-1 response rates and immune phenotypes across six cancer types.

props.command:
claude "Compile single-agent anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-L1
response rates across six cancer types: Hodgkin
lymphoma, melanoma, MSI-high colorectal, NSCLC
PD-L1 high, triple-negative breast cancer,
pancreatic adenocarcinoma. For each: ORR, median
TMB, PD-L1 expression level, immune phenotype
(hot/excluded/cold), and leading reason for
non-response. Cite the pivotal trial."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) -- Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code. The script builds the six-cancer comparison table sorted by ORR descending — cancer, ORR, TMB, phenotype, and resistance reason in one view.

props.code:
# checkpoint_table.py -- Claude Code output
CANCERS = [
  {"name": "Hodgkin lymphoma", "orr": 0.72,
   "tmb": "low", "phenotype": "hot",
   "resistance": "PD-L1 amplification"},
  {"name": "Melanoma", "orr": 0.40,
   "tmb": "high", "phenotype": "hot",
   "resistance": "T cell exhaustion"},
  {"name": "MSI-high CRC", "orr": 0.36,
   "tmb": "high", "phenotype": "hot",
   "resistance": "beta-2-microglobulin loss"},
  {"name": "NSCLC PD-L1 hi", "orr": 0.30,
   "tmb": "moderate", "phenotype": "excluded",
   "resistance": "KRAS co-mutation"},
  {"name": "TNBC", "orr": 0.18,
   "tmb": "low", "phenotype": "cold",
   "resistance": "immunosuppressive TME"},
  {"name": "Pancreatic", "orr": 0.04,
   "tmb": "low", "phenotype": "cold",
   "resistance": "desmoplastic stroma"},
]
for c in sorted(CANCERS, key=lambda x: -x['orr']):
  print(f"{c['name']:<22} ORR={int(c['orr']*100)}% {c['phenotype']:<10} {c['resistance']}")
props.title: checkpoint_table.py

beats.narration_text: Six horizontal bars ordered by ORR. Hodgkin at seventy-two percent in CRIMSON. Melanoma forty percent. MSI-high colorectal thirty-six percent. NSCLC thirty percent. TNBC eighteen percent. Pancreatic four percent in SLATE. The color marks the hot/cold distinction. The gap is fifteen-fold.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: six horizontal bars ordered highest to lowest ORR; Hodgkin CRIMSON; pancreatic SLATE

beats.narration_text: Now ask the hard question: has any cold-to-hot conversion strategy moved pancreatic cancer response?

props.command:
claude "Has any cold-to-hot conversion strategy (STING
agonists, oncolytic viruses, radiation before
checkpoint blockade) produced a meaningful
improvement in pancreatic cancer checkpoint
response rates in a phase 2 trial?"

beats.narration_text: The same chart with a pancreatic annotation. Combination strategies — STING agonists, oncolytic virus, radiation plus checkpoint — all in Phase 1 to 2 only. No Phase 3 improvement yet. The pancreatic bar does not grow. The cold tumor remains cold.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same chart + pancreatic note: no Phase 2 improvement from cold-to-hot strategies

beats.narration_text: TMB and PD-L1 are correlates not causes. The mechanistic driver is whether T cells were ever primed against tumor-specific antigens and whether the tumor is suppressing them with a reversible mechanism. Hodgkin works because PD-L1 is amplified on the Reed-Sternberg cells — a reversible suppression. Pancreatic fails because the stroma physically excludes T cells.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: correlates vs causes; reversible vs structural suppression

beats.narration_text: Before any checkpoint inhibitor, verify three separate tests: PD-L1 expression, MSI status, and TMB. They predict non-overlapping subsets of responders. A tumor that is PD-L1 negative may still respond via MSI. A tumor that is MSI-stable may still respond via high TMB.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: three tests (PD-L1, MSI, TMB); non-overlapping responder subsets

beats.narration_text: Humanitarians AI. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Humanitarians AI, nikbearbrown dot com.
